elements.sent-2411-001: State-of-the-art hardware compilers for FPGAs often fail to find efficient mappings of high-level designs to low-level primitives, especially complex programmable primitives like digital signal processors (DSPs).
elements.sent-2411-002: However, this approach has two primary drawbacks.
elements.sent-2411-003: First, sketch-guided program synthesis requires the user to provide sketches, which are challenging to write and require domain expertise.
elements.sent-2411-004: Second, the open-source SMT solvers which power sketch-guided program synthesis struggle with the sorts of operations common in hardware--- namely multiplication.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this paper, we address both of these challenges using an equality saturation (eqsat) framework.
elements.sent-2411-006: By combining eqsat and an existing state-of-the-art program-synthesis-based tool, we produce Churchroad, a technology mapper which handles larger and more complex designs than the program-synthesis-based tool alone, while eliminating the need for a user to provide sketches.
elements.sent-2411-007: State of the art FPGA hardware synthesis tools such as Xilinx's Vivado or the open-source Yosys~\cite{wolf2013yosys often fail to fully utilize the features of complex, programmable FPGA primitives like DSPs~\cite{lakeroad .}}
elements.sent-2411-008: For example, they might fail to pack multiple operations (e.g.~a multiply followed by an add) onto a single DSP, instead using a DSP and additional logic resources.
elements.sent-2411-009: Our recent tool Lakeroad~\cite{lakeroad uses sketch-guided program synthesis to more fully take advantage of all features of programmable primitives.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Sketch-guided program synthesis is a technique which uses SMT solvers to completely search through a space for a solution--- in Lakeroad's case, searching through possible DSP configurations.
elements.sent-2411-011: This technique allows Lakeroad to more thoroughly support all features of DSPs.
elements.sent-2411-012: However, Lakeroad suffers from two primary limitations.
elements.sent-2411-014: Lakeroad requires users to provide sketches~\cite{sketchPhdThesis .}
elements.sent-2411-015: These sketches capture a rough outline of the compiled design and guide the SMT solvers in filling the ``holes'', i.e., missing parts of the design.
elements.sent-2411-016: Writing sketches is both tedious and difficult, requiring knowledge of the sketching DSL in addition to domain expertise of the target being compiled to.
elements.sent-2411-018: The open-source SMT solvers used to power sketch-guided synthesis do not scale due to the inherent inefficiency of the underlying bit-blasting algorithm typically used in SMT solvers~\cite{bitblasting_wrong_path .}
elements.sent-2411-019: This prevents Lakeroad from compiling larger designs or supporting operations like multiplication over large bitwidths.
elements.sent-2411-020: Cite control logic synthesis paper from zach and jon, cite lakeroad.
elements.sent-2411-021: This paper mitigates both problems with a key insight: a program-synthesis-based tool like Lakeroad should be viewed as a specialized, high-powered subroutine which should only be called on portions of the design; furthermore, to orchestrate calls to specialized subroutines like Lakeroad, we can use a framework called equality saturation (or eqsat)~\cite{egg2021,egglog,smith2024there .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Eqsat is a term rewriting technique built around a core data structure called an \egraph~\cite{nelsonPhD .}
elements.sent-2411-023: This divide-and-conquer approach based on eqsat and \egraph{ s helps mitigate both aforementioned problems.}
elements.sent-2411-024: First, by facilitating the application of semantics-preserving rewrite rules~\cite{eqsat2009, egg2021 , we can break down large designs (e.g.~wide multipliers) and compile them piece by piece, leading to more tractable problems for the SMT solvers underlying Lakeroad.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Second, we can eliminate the need for users to provide sketches, by instead inferring the structure of the output from information inside the \egraph.
elements.sent-2411-026: We showcase this idea in Churchroad, a new, open-source\footnote{ Churchroad is located at \url{https://github.com/gussmith23/churchroad .. technology mapper.}}
elements.sent-2411-027: Churchroad is built upon the egglog~\cite{egglog eqsat framework, and calls out to Lakeroad as a specialized subroutine.}
elements.sent-2411-028: In the rest of this paper, we demonstrate with a detailed example the core ideas behind Churchroad.
elements.sent-2411-029: To motivate Churchroad and to explain how it works, we use a running example.
elements.sent-2411-030: We first describe our example design, and describe how it should be optimally compiled onto a Xilinx UltraScale+ FPGA.
elements.sent-2411-031: We then explain how a single query to Lakeroad will fail to compile this design.
elements.sent-2411-032: Finally, we explain how Churchroad uses eqsat and \egraph{ s to break the mapping task into multiple simpler queries to Lakeroad.}
elements.sent-2411-033: Consider a multiplier which multiplies a 16-bit number $a$ with a 32-bit number $b$ and produces the lower 32 bits of the result (all unsigned): \begin{minted [fontsize= ] {systemverilog module mul(input [15:0] a, input [31:0] b, output [31:0] o); assign o = a * b; endmodule \end{minted We refer to this high-level, pre-compilation implementation of the design as the specification or spec.}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: From this point on, we will refer to the spec with math instead of Verilog.
elements.sent-2411-035: Thus, our spec is: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:spec} Spec\left(a, b\right) := a \times b \ \left(\text{or}\ a \times \left(b_1 \mdoubleplus b_0\right)\right)\end{equation} To map this design onto a Xilinx UltraScale+ FPGA, it would be ideal to use the UltraScale+'s specialized DSP48E2 primitive, which efficiently implements multiplication.
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:spec} Spec\left(a, b\right) := a \times b \ \left(or\ a \times \left(b_1 \mdoubleplus b_0\right)\right)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-036: Implementing our design requires two DSP48E2s, arranged in the following way: \begin{center \includegraphics[width=.6\linewidth]{figures/dsp_impl.drawio.png \end{center where DSP0 computes the lower 16 bits of the multiplication, $\left(a \times b_0\right)^{15 _0$, and DSP1 implements the upper 16 bits of the multiplication, $\left(a \times b_1 + (a \times b_0)\gg 16\right)^{15 _0$ (extraction of the lower 16 bits is implicit in the diagram), taking advantage of the DSP's internal shifter\footnote{ In reality the DSP48E2 uses a 17-bit internal shift; we use 16 simply for rounder numbers in our example.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Our previous work~\cite{lakeroad demonstrated that existing open-source, pattern-matching-based technology mappers such as those in Yosys~\cite{wolf2013yosys struggle to utilize all features of complex, programmable primitives like DSPs.}}
elements.sent-2411-038: In response, we introduced Lakeroad, an open-source technology mapper which can more completely use the features of DSPs.
elements.sent-2411-039: However, Lakeroad was only evaluated on small designs which map to single DSPs; in fact, Lakeroad struggles with designs requiring multiple DSPs.
elements.sent-2411-040: We will now explain exactly how Lakeroad will struggle to compile this design, to understand the core challenges which Churchroad solves.
elements.sent-2411-041: A sketch is an outline of the compiled result, with holes which Lakeroad must fill in to produce a completed design.
elements.sent-2411-042: To compile our example design, we need a sketch which captures the general structure of our 2-DSP output.
elements.sent-2411-043: This sketch currently does not exist within Lakeroad, however, which is problem 1: users may need to provide their own sketches, which can be tricky to write and require domain knowledge of what the compiled result should look like.
elements.sent-2411-044: In mathematical notation, our sketch looks like: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:sketch}  Sketch \left(p_0, p_1, a, b\right) := let\ b_1, b_0 := b^{31}_{16}, b^{15}_0 DSP0 :=DSP\left(p_0, a, b_0\right) DSP1 :=DSP\left(p_1, a, b_1, DSP0\right) in \ DSP1^{15}_0 \mdoubleplus DSP0^{15}_0 \end{equation} Lakeroad then fills in the holes of the sketch, such that the output implements the spec.
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:sketch}  Sketch \left(p_0, p_1, a, b\right) := let\ b_1, b_0 := b^{31}_{16}, b^{15}_0 DSP0 :=DSP\left(p_0, a, b_0\right) DSP1 :=DSP\left(p_1, a, b_1, DSP0\right) in \ DSP1^{15}_0 \mdoubleplus DSP0^{15}_0 \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-045: Specifically, Lakeroad solves the following query: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:synth-query} \exists p_0, p_1.\ \forall a, b.\ Spec\left(a,b\right) =Sketch\left(p_0, p_1, a, b\right)\end{equation} That is, it searches for a configuration of the two DSPs (captured by $p_0$ and $p_1$) such that $\text{DSP1 ^{15 _0 \mdoubleplus \text{DSP0 ^{15 _0$ is equivalent to our spec, $a\times b$.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:synth-query} \exists p_0, p_1.\ \forall a, b.\ Spec\left(a,b\right) =Sketch\left(p_0, p_1, a, b\right)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-046: To find such a configuration, Lakeroad's sketch guided synthesis algorithm (implemented by Rosette~\cite{torlak2013growing ) first guesses at values of $p_0$ and $p_1$, and then uses an SMT solver to verify whether the guesses are correct.}
elements.sent-2411-047: To do so, it queries an SMT solver, asking $\forall a, b.\ Spec\left(a, b\right)= Sketch\left(p_0, p_1, a, b\right)$ for the guessed values of $p_0$ and $p_1$.
elements.sent-2411-048: Continuing our example, imagine that the program synthesis algorithm chooses a $p_0$ and $p_1$ such that $\forall a, b.\ Spec\left(a, b\right)= Sketch\left(p_0, p_1, a, b\right)$ expands to \begin{equation}\label{eqn:simplified-synth-query} \forall a, b.\ a\times b= \left(a \times b_1 + (a \times b_0)\gg 16\right)^{15}_0 \mdoubleplus \left(a \times b_0\right)^{15}_0\end{equation} If the SMT solver can prove this to be true, then $p_0$ and $p_1$ can be used to configure our sketches and produce an output design.
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:simplified-synth-query} \forall a, b.\ a\times b= \left(a \times b_1 + (a \times b_0)\gg 16\right)^{15}_0 \mdoubleplus \left(a \times b_0\right)^{15}_0\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-049: If the SMT solver instead returns a counterexample--- that is, values of $a$ and $b$ for which \cref{eqn:simplified-synth-query is not true--- it can use the counterexample to pick new guesses for $p_0$ and $p_1$.}
elements.sent-2411-050: However, when we attempt to prove \cref{eqn:simplified-synth-query with open-source SMT solvers, they take an infeasible amount of time proving this query--- problem 2 in the introduction.}
elements.sent-2411-051: To demonstrate this, we implement the query directly within the SMT solver-aided programming language, Rosette~\cite{torlak2013growing :.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=.99\linewidth]{figures/mul_verify_timeout_experiment.png} \caption{Solver time to prove \cref{eqn:simplified-synth-query} with Rosette, at various settings of bw and with various solvers. Timeout is set to 10 seconds.} \label{fig:timeout}
elements.sent-2411-052: At the root of the problem is multiplication.
elements.sent-2411-053: The underlying bitblasting~\cite{bitblasting_algo algorithm converts the query into first-order logic using low-level $\vee$/$\wedge$/$\neg$ operators.}
elements.sent-2411-054: This conversion causes slowdowns when faced with bitwise-complex operations like multiplication~\cite{bitblasting_wrong_path .}
elements.sent-2411-055: Churchroad's first fundamental insight is to mitigate SMT timeouts by breaking up large queries into multiple smaller queries that are easier for SMT solvers to handle.
elements.sent-2411-056: For example, if we can break \cref{eqn:synth-query into two queries, \begin{equation} \exists p_0.\ \forall a,b.\ a \times b_0 = DSP(p_0, a, b)\ and \exists p_1.\ \forall a,b,c.\ a \times b_1 + c \gg 16 = DSP(p_1, a, b, c) \end{equation} then, after the synthesis procedure picks concrete guesses for $p_0$ and $p_1$, we might end up with queries like \begin{equation} \forall a,b.\ a \times b_0 = a \times b_0 \ and \forall a,b,c.\ a \times b_1 + c \gg 16 =a \times b_1 + c \gg 16 \end{equation} which are trivial for an SMT solver to prove.}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation} \exists p_0.\ \forall a,b.\ a \times b_0 = DSP(p_0, a, b)\ and \exists p_1.\ \forall a,b,c.\ a \times b_1 + c \gg 16 = DSP(p_1, a, b, c) \end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation} \forall a,b.\ a \times b_0 = a \times b_0 \ and \forall a,b,c.\ a \times b_1 + c \gg 16 =a \times b_1 + c \gg 16 \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-057: To break up queries, Churchroad pre-applies equalities we know to be true.
elements.sent-2411-058: In this example, we'd like to apply the equality in \cref{eqn:simplified-synth-query to rewrite the spec $a \times b$ into its expanded form, with the eventual goal of compiling its subexpressions.}
elements.sent-2411-059: This equality is not just specific to our example--- it is true in general, and is in Churchroad's database of equalities.
elements.sent-2411-060: To apply these equalities, Churchroad utilizes the egglog eqsat framework~\cite{egglog , which provides a data structure called an \egraph to hold our design.}
elements.sent-2411-061: We can then capture equalities like \cref{eqn:simplified-synth-query as rules over the \egraph: \begin{minted [fontsize= ]{text (rule ((= e (Mul arg0 arg1)) (= (bitwidth arg0) 16) (= (bitwidth arg1) 32)) ((union e (Concat (Extract 15 0 (Add (Mul arg0 (Extract 31 16 arg1)) (Shr (Mul arg0 (Extract 15 0 arg1)) 16))) (Extract 15 0 (Mul arg0 (Extract 15 0 arg1))))))) \end{minted egglog rules takes two arguments: first, a list of expressions to search for in the \egraph, and second, a list of commands to run if those expressions are found.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: In this case, this rule looks for a multiplication expression, which it names e, and checks the bitwidths of the arguments arg0 and arg1.
elements.sent-2411-063: For this example, we have hardcoded this rule to search for specific bitwidths, but the actual rule in Churchroad works for variable bitwidths.
elements.sent-2411-064: If an expression e matching the pattern is found, the rule unions it with the expression in the right-hand side of \cref{eqn:simplified-synth-query .}
elements.sent-2411-065: To understand what we mean by union, let's see what this rewrite does to our spec.
elements.sent-2411-066: When we apply this rule to our spec (left), it matches on the Mul node, and produces the \egraph on the right: \begin{center \includegraphics[width=.7\linewidth]{figures/egraph1and2.drawio.png \end{center We have simplified the \egraph by dropping some extracts.}}}
elements.sent-2411-067: Note the dotted box around the top-level Mul and Concat nodes.
elements.sent-2411-068: This indicates that, as a result of the union command in our rule above, these nodes are in the same equivalence class or eclass within the \egraph, and are thus equivalent.
elements.sent-2411-069: The dotted boxes around the other nodes indicate that they are the only nodes in their eclasses.
elements.sent-2411-070: When the \egraph contained only (Mul a b), the only mapping we could attempt was our ill-fated 2-DSP mapping.
elements.sent-2411-071: Furthermore, attempting this mapping required explicit user input: the user needed to provide the sketch in \cref{eqn:sketch .}
elements.sent-2411-072: These sketches are challenging to write, and require the user to have some idea of what the output design should look like (problem 1).
elements.sent-2411-073: Churchroad's second fundamental insight is that we can entirely remove the need for user-provided sketches.
elements.sent-2411-074: Instead of requiring the user to provide a sketch of the structure of the output, we can instead infer the structure of the output from information already within the \egraph.
elements.sent-2411-075: To infer the structure of the compiled output, Churchroad searches through the \egraph for expressions that look like they can be implemented using a DSP.
elements.sent-2411-076: For this, Churchroad again uses egglog rules: \begin{minted [fontsize= ]{text (rule ((= e (Mul arg0 arg1)) (<= (bw e) 48) (<= (bw arg0) 17) (<= (bw arg1) 17)) ((union e (DSP? arg0 arg1)))) (rule ((= e (Add (Mul arg0 arg1) (Shl arg2 const))) (<= (bw e) 48) ...}}
elements.sent-2411-077: The first rule simply searches for multiplies; the second, for multiply--adds with a shift on the input.
elements.sent-2411-078: Any time these rules fire, they insert a DSP proposal node, ``DSP?''.
elements.sent-2411-079: These nodes simply mark that the eclass may be implementable using a DSP, and that Lakeroad should be used to determine whether such a mapping exists.
elements.sent-2411-080: These rules can fire multiple times on the \egraph above, resulting in the following \egraph: \begin{center \includegraphics[width=.5\linewidth]{figures/egraph3.drawio.png \end{center Each eclass with a DSP?~node has been identified by our rules above as potentially implementable using a DSP.}}}
elements.sent-2411-081: To confirm whether or not each eclass can actually be implemented with a DSP, Churchroad now calls Lakeroad as a subroutine on each proposed DSP?~node.
elements.sent-2411-082: Let's begin with the eclass labeled 1.
elements.sent-2411-083: Recall that Lakeroad requires both a spec and a sketch, both of which were provided by the user.
elements.sent-2411-084: However, Churchroad is able to generate both specs and sketches automatically, from the information in the \egraph.
elements.sent-2411-085: This eclass contains two nodes: one node representing a subexpression of the rewritten spec, $a \times b_0$, and $\text{DSP?}\left(a, b_0\right)$ representing a potential DSP implementation of this eclass.
elements.sent-2411-086: The first node serves as our spec, while the second serves as our sketch! Thus, the synthesis query sent to Lakeroad looks like \begin{equation}\exists p.\ \forall a,b.\ a \times b_0 =\text{DSP}\left(p, a, b_0\right)\end{equation} where the sketch $\text{DSP}\left(p, a, b_0\right)$ was generated by adding parameters $p$ to the original ``DSP?'' node.
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}\exists p.\ \forall a,b.\ a \times b_0 =DSP\left(p, a, b_0\right)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-087: Note that this is much simpler than the query in \cref{eqn:synth-query ; consequently, the underlying SMT solver is able to solve it, and Lakeroad is able to generate a configuration of the UltraScale+ DSP48E2 which implements $a \times b_0$, which is then inserted into the \egraph.}
elements.sent-2411-088: We can repeat this process for the eclass labeled 2, which produces the following synthesis query: \begin{equation}\exists p.\ \forall a,b.\ a \times b_1 + (a \times b_0)\gg 16 = \text{DSP}\left(p, a, b_1, a \times b_0\right)\end{equation} Again, Lakeroad returns a DSP mapping for this query, which we can insert into the \egraph.
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}\exists p.\ \forall a,b.\ a \times b_1 + (a \times b_0)\gg 16 = DSP\left(p, a, b_1, a \times b_0\right)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-089: The final step is to extract a compiled design from the \egraph.
elements.sent-2411-090: Currently, Churchroad does this in a straightforward manner: for each eclass, it simply chooses a node which is legal in structural Verilog.
elements.sent-2411-091: For example, module instantiations, zero-/sign-extensions, extractions, and concatenations are allowed, while behavioral operators like Mul are not.
elements.sent-2411-092: In this case, we extract our two DSP instantiations produced by Lakeroad.
elements.sent-2411-093: Finally, Churchroad converts from the internal representation extracted from the \egraph to structural Verilog, producing the design in the block diagram at the beginning of the section.
elements.sent-2411-094: From beginning to end, Churchroad currently takes about 4 seconds to compile this example.
elements.sent-2411-095: With that, Churchroad has successfully compiled a design which Lakeroad could not handle.
elements.sent-2411-096: Using eqsat and encoding and certain equalities like \cref{eqn:simplified-synth-query as axioms, Churchroad expanded the initial spec.}
elements.sent-2411-097: Then, using DSP proposal rules, Churchroad found locations in the expanded spec which might be implementable using a DSP.
elements.sent-2411-098: Finally, these DSP proposals were sent to Lakeroad, which produced mappings to DSPs.
elements.sent-2411-099: Finally, with all of the Lakeroad-produced mappings stored in the \egraph, Churchroad extracted the final compiled design.
elements.sent-2411-100: Sisco et al.~\cite{zachsisco used sketch-guided program synthesis for ``loop rerolling''---this enabled decompilation of low-level netlist to generate high-level HDL.}
elements.sent-2411-101: Other papers have also explored the use of sketch-guided synthesis for generating HDL implementations from high-level designs~\cite{verisketch, sketchilog .}
elements.sent-2411-102: Tools like ODIN~\cite{odin and ODIN-II~\cite{odin2 rely on syntactic matching to map portions of a design to specialized units.}}
elements.sent-2411-103: Brain et al.~\cite{bitblasting_wrong_path demonstrated that bit-blasting can be optimized by changing the multiplier encoding using either; (i) constant propagation and rewrites; or (ii) polynomial interpolation.}
elements.sent-2411-104: Rath et al.~\cite{polysat have presented an alternative to bit-blasting with PolySAT by providing a theory-solver for bit-vector arithmetic using non-linear polynomials and a bit-vector plugin to the equality graph.}
elements.sent-2411-105: Yu et al.~\cite{formal_verification_function_extraction 's work enables formal verification of large arithmetic circuits.}
elements.sent-2411-106: There is also a functional verification technique for multipliers presented by Yu et al.~\cite{verification_galois_field that uses Galois field (GF) arithmetic to verify an n-bit GF multiplier in n threads, with experimental results up to 571 bits.}
elements.sent-2411-107: Dafny supports user-defined axioms to prevent SMT solver timeouts~\cite{dafny-ref .}
elements.sent-2411-108: Haploid~\cite{haploid is another tool that uses axioms to simplify SMT queries in a preprocessing step using equality saturation.}
elements.sent-2411-109: This has applications for formally verifying circuits, and experiences similar SAT/SMT blow-ups you experienced.
elements.sent-2411-110: There's a relevant line of work I wanted to bring up which uses an algebraic approach to represent circuits as polynomials in a finite field (GF2), and rewrite them to find multipliers (not eq-sat).
elements.sent-2411-111: Happy to add a blurb and citations about this if you have a related work section.
elements.sent-2411-113: This work provides early evidence that equational reasoning using \egraphs can help scale program synthesis-based technology mapping techniques.
elements.sent-2411-114: We demonstrated this idea in the form of a new tool, Churchroad, which uses Lakeroad as one specialized subroutine that focuses on specific portions of the design.
elements.sent-2411-115: Using equational reasoning, Churchroad breaks down larger designs, and thus shrinks the queries sent to Lakeroad while eliminating the need for user-provided sketches.
elements.sent-2411-116: In the future, we are eager to demonstrate how \egraphs can be used to orchestrate more tools like Yosys, Lakeroad, ABC, etc. to further improve technology mapping.
elements.sent-2411-117: We also plan to generate correct rewrite rules for Churchroad, rather than writing them manually.
elements.sent-2411-118: We thank Zachary D.~Sisco, Vishal Canumalla, and Jin Yang for feedback on our work and Thanawat Techaumnuaiwit for helping with the implementation.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}
